title: Λέων καὶ μῦς ἀντευεργέτης
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 206 (= Perry 150). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Λέοντος κοιμωμένου μῦς τῷ σώματι ἐπέδραμεν.
sentences.tr: Leontos koimōmenou mys tōi sōmati epedramen.
sentences.l: A lion sleeping, a mouse over-the body ran.
sentences.i: While a lion slept, a mouse ran across his body.
sentences.n: The whole opening scene is a two-word genitive absolute: Λέοντος κοιμωμένου, 'a lion sleeping.' The title's ἀντευεργέτης means 'benefactor in return' — the fable's point compressed into one compound word.

sentences.t: Ὁ δὲ ἐξαναστὰς καὶ συλλαβὼν αὐτὸν οἷός τε ἦν καταθοινήσασθαι.
sentences.tr: Ho de exanastas kai syllabōn auton hoios te ēn katathoinēsasthai.
sentences.l: And he, having-started-up and having-seized him, was-such-as to-make-a-feast-of him.
sentences.i: The lion started up, seized him, and was about to devour him.
sentences.n: οἷός τε ἦν — literally 'was such as (to)': the standard idiom for 'was able, was ready to.' καταθοινήσασθαι, 'make a θοίνη (banquet) of' — the predator's verb; the wolf will use it of the lamb too.

sentences.t: Τοῦ δὲ δεηθέντος μεθεῖναι αὐτὸν καὶ λέγοντος ὅτι σωθεὶς χάριτας αὐτῷ ἀποδώσει, γελάσας ἀπέλυσεν αὐτόν.
sentences.tr: Tou de deēthentos metheinai auton kai legontos hoti sōtheis charitas autōi apodōsei, gelasas apelysen auton.
sentences.l: And he having-begged to-release him and saying that, saved, thanks to-him he-will-repay, having-laughed he-let-him-go.
sentences.i: The mouse begged to be released, promising that if spared he would repay the favor — and the lion, laughing, let him go.
sentences.n: χάριτας ἀποδώσει, 'will give back charis' — χάρις means both 'favor' and 'gratitude,' and the whole fable turns on that double sense. Note the future ἀποδώσει kept inside the ὅτι-clause: Greek reports the promise in the promiser's own tense.

sentences.t: Συνέβη δὲ αὐτὸν μετ' οὐ πολὺ τῇ τοῦ μυὸς χάριτι περισωθῆναι·
sentences.tr: Synebē de auton met' ou poly tēi tou myos chariti perisōthēnai:
sentences.l: And it-happened that-he, after not much, by-the of-the mouse gratitude was-saved-through:
sentences.i: And not long after, it so fell out that the mouse's gratitude saved his life.
sentences.n: συνέβη + accusative + infinitive — 'it befell that…' — for the third time in these fables; you own this construction now. μετ' οὐ πολύ: 'after not much (time)' — understatement by negation.

sentences.t: ἐπειδὴ γὰρ συλληφθεὶς ὑπό τινων κυνηγετῶν κάλῳ ἐδέθη τινὶ δένδρῳ, τὸ τηνικαῦτα ἀκούσας ὁ μῦς αὐτοῦ στένοντος ἐλθὼν τὸν κάλων περιέτρωγε καὶ λύσας αὐτὸν ἔφη·
sentences.tr: epeidē gar syllēphtheis hypo tinōn kynēgetōn kalōi edethē tini dendrōi, to tēnikauta akousas ho mys autou stenontos elthōn ton kalōn perietrōge kai lysas auton ephē:
sentences.l: for when, having-been-caught by certain hunters, with-a-rope he-was-bound to-a-certain tree, thereupon the mouse, having-heard him groaning, having-come, the rope gnawed-around and, having-freed him, said:
sentences.i: For the lion was caught by hunters and bound to a tree with a rope; and the mouse, hearing his groans, came and gnawed the rope through, freed him, and said:
sentences.n: ἀκούσας αὐτοῦ στένοντος — verbs of hearing take the genitive of the person heard, with the participle painting the sound: 'heard him groaning.' The imperfect περιέτρωγε is the gnawing in progress, bite after bite, before the aorist λύσας snaps the rope.

sentences.t: « Σὺ μὲν οὕτω μου τότε κατεγέλασας ὡς μὴ προσδεχόμενος παρ' ἐμοῦ ἀμοιβὴν κομιεῖσθαι· νῦν δὲ εὖ ἴσθι ὅτι ἐστὶ καὶ παρὰ μυσὶ χάρις. »
sentences.tr: « Sy men houtō mou tote kategelasas hōs mē prosdechomenos par' emou amoibēn komieisthai: nyn de eu isthi hoti esti kai para mysi charis. »
sentences.l: « You then so laughed-at me, as not expecting from me a repayment to-receive: but now well know that there-is even among mice gratitude. »
sentences.i: « You laughed at me then, never expecting any repayment from a mouse; but know now that there is gratitude even among mice. »
sentences.n: καταγελάω takes the genitive (μου): 'laugh at me.' εὖ ἴσθι — 'know well,' the imperative of οἶδα. The fable's last word is χάρις.

sentences.t: Ὁ λόγος δηλοῖ ὅτι καιρῶν μεταβολαῖς οἱ σφόδρα δυνατοὶ τῶν ἀσθενεστέρων ἐνδεεῖς γίνονται.
sentences.tr: Ho logos dēloi hoti kairōn metabolais hoi sphodra dynatoi tōn asthenesterōn endeeis ginontai.
sentences.l: The story shows that in-changes of-times the exceedingly powerful of-the weaker in-need become.
sentences.i: The story shows that when fortune turns, the mightiest come to need the weaker.
sentences.n: καιρῶν μεταβολαῖς — a bare dative of circumstance: 'amid the changes of times.' ἐνδεής takes the genitive: 'in need of the weaker.'
